title: Blueberry and lemon scones
subtitle: Buttermilk scones folded around fresh blueberries with lemon zest
excerpt: Twelve scones with fresh blueberries folded into the dough and a lemon glaze brushed over the warm tops. The buttermilk keeps the crumb tender, the cold butter keeps the layers distinct, and the blueberries weep just enough purple juice through the dough to mark each scone.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136,the basic scone template (rubbing butter into flour, light buttermilk binding). Florence White, Good Things in England (1932),the West Country and Welsh scone variants. The fold-in-fruit method, particularly with summer berries, is a twentieth-century North American addition; the lemon glaze is a finishing-touch borrowed from American buttermilk biscuits. The buttermilk-to-flour ratio (300 ml to 500 g) is the standard British anchor.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked scones on the tray; bag once solid and bake from frozen for 18 minutes. Best within 6 weeks.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 24,bake on two trays staggered if your oven is small.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rub the butter into the flour the night before; cover and chill the dry mix.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, top shelf. Conventional 230°C, middle shelf. The high heat sets the outside fast and locks the lift.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of working cold butter into flour with the fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with a few larger flakes left visible. The fat coats the flour, restricts gluten development, and leaves cold pockets that flash to steam in the oven,that is the lift in a scone or shortcrust. Work fast and keep your hands cool; warm butter melts into the flour and the layers collapse.

Pressing a scone cutter straight down and lifting it straight up,without twisting,is the rule. A twist seals the cut edge of the dough, stops the layers from rising freely, and produces a stunted, lopsided scone. If a cutter sticks, dust it again in flour and try once more.

content.text: These are buttermilk scones with fresh blueberries folded through the dough and a thin lemon glaze brushed over the warm tops. Twelve scones, around 30 minutes from bowl to plate. The buttermilk gives the crumb its tenderness; the cold butter gives the lift; the blueberries weep just enough through the dough to mark each scone purple at the edges.
Three points hold the scone together. Work cold,the butter, the buttermilk, and your hands. Mix little,overmixed dough toughens. Cut clean,press the cutter straight down without twisting.
Whisk the flour, baking powder, salt, and sugar together in a large bowl. Add the cold cubed butter and
with cool fingertips until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs with a few flat flakes of butter still visible. Stir the lemon zest through.

Make a well in the centre and pour in the cold buttermilk. Cut the liquid into the dry mix with a butter knife in 6 to 8 strokes,just until no dry flour shows. Scatter the blueberries over the surface and fold them in with 4 more strokes. The dough is shaggy and uneven; do not knead it.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan (230°C conventional) and line a baking tray with paper. Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat,do not roll,into a rectangle 3 cm thick. The patting protects the butter layers.
Dip the cutter in flour and
press straight down and lift straight up

,no twisting. Cut as close together as you can. Lift each scone onto the tray with a palette knife, leaving 3 cm between. Re-roll the scraps once, gently; cut the last scones from those. Chill the tray in the fridge for 15 minutes while the oven finishes heating,the cold rest tightens the butter and improves the lift.

Brush the tops lightly with milk,no egg, the wash should be matt. Bake on the top shelf of a fan oven for 12 to 14 minutes, until the tops are golden, the sides have set firm, and the scones have risen tall and straight. The blueberries will be staining the dough at the edges.

While the scones bake, whisk the icing sugar with the lemon juice until smooth. The glaze should pour from a spoon in a thick ribbon,add a teaspoon of water if it is too stiff. As the scones come out, brush the warm tops with the glaze; it will set firm as they cool. Eat the same day, split warm with butter or clotted cream.
